comments.text: An observation: by the Ajtai–Komlós–Szemerédi independence number bound and Shearer's refinement, every triangle-free graph G on n vertices satisfies F(G) ≥ Ω(√(n log n)). The argument extends to K_r-free graphs for any fixed r ≥ 3. The conjecture therefore reduces to graphs where ω(G) → ∞
well if $\omega(G) = o(\log n)$ then by offdiagonal ramsey bounds you would win.

Paul Dyson and I have been working hard on this problem and just posted a paper on the arXiv: Paul W. Dyson and Brendan D. McKay, Ramsey numbers for regular induced subgraphs, arXiv:2604.08215 (2026). Among other things, we improved the $n^{1/2}(\log n)^{3/4}$ upper bound of Alon, Krivelevich, and Sudakov to $\sqrt{163 n/9}$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Some of the OEIS sequences listed in our paper could be added to the "Related OEIS Sequences" section of this webpage. A394564 Least integer a(n) such that every graph on a(n) vertices has an induced regular subgraph of order n. A394574 Greatest a(n) such that every graph on n vertices has an induced regular subgraph of order a(n). A394563 Least integer a(n) such that every graph on a(n) vertices has an induced subgraph of order at least n. A390257 Minimum size of maximum regular induced subgraph of a graph on n vertices. (Already listed on this webpage.) A394573 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 4. A394400 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 4 or greater. A394539 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 5. A390919 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 5 or greater. A394462 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 6. A392636 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 6 or greater. A394930 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 7. A394933 Number of graphs with n vertices that have no induced regular subgraph of order 7 or greater. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Working together with Paul Dyson, I now have graphs of order 29 with no regular subgraph of order 7. Thus $G(7) \ge 30$. More information will be provided when it is available.

At https://users.cecs.anu.edu.au/~bdm/data/ramsey.html I have put all the extremal graphs for regular induced subgraphs of order at least 4 (10 on 6 vertices), and order at least 5 (954 on 16 vertices). I also provided the following: * 2,808,915 graphs of order 20 with no regular subgraph of order 6. This is certainly not a complete collection but my computer got tired. I have verified that none of these extend to 21 vertices. * 158,274 graphs of order 28 with no regular subgraph of order 7. This is also certainly not a complete collection. In this case I have NOT verified that none extend to 29 vertices as it would take my naive extender too long. If anyone has a clever idea on how to do this test quickly, I'd be very interested.

Brendan.
I assume you mean $G(6)$?

Define $G(k)=\min\{ n : F(n)\ge k \}$, that is $G(k)$ is the smallest order on which every graph has a regular induced subgraph of order $k$ or greater. This is the same way Ramsey numbers are defined, and I find it easier on the brain. $G$ determines $F$ due to monotonicity. In 1997, I computed G(2)=2, G(3)=5, G(4)=7, G(5)=17 but didn't publish it. I just repeated that computation now with fresh code and got the same result. The lexicographic product $P_4[P_4]$ is a short proof that $G(5)\ge 17$ but I don't know of a non-computational proof that $G(5)=17$. Here are the counts, from 1 vertex to 16 vertices, of graphs which do not have a regular induced subgraph of order 5 or more: 1, 2, 4, 11, 31, 130, 728, 6027, 66308, 818276, 8336902, 45933753, 79888458, 23814804, 512906, 954. I have examples to show that $G(6)\ge 19$ and $G(7)\ge 28$ and I'm still searching for larger examples. I don't know why there should be a small leap from $G(5)$ to $G(6)$ then a large leap from $G(6)$ to $G(7)$, but the fact that there are 8 regular graphs of order 6 compared to only 6 of order 7 might have something to do with it My simple code for $G(5)$ took about 25 hours to show $G(5)=17$ but it cannot find $G(6)$ in a reasonable amount of time. I'm thinking about how to design a smarter algorithm. (Brendan McKay).
Great, thanks Brendan, and welcome to the site!

There is some discussion on GitHub regarding computing this function: https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/issues/94 My computation of $G(5)=17$ is finishing up. The count for order 16 graphs agrees. I am using nauty. Perhaps of most interest is this graph which demonstrates $G(6)\ge 20$: R?AAD@OiEg\O\A|c[Zew\eV^E~v~fw Thank you for sharing your results, and for nauty.
Now I also found a few graphs to show $G(6)\ge 20$, plus some for $G(7)\ge 29$.
Out of curiosity, do you know how long the computation took in 1997?

I think it was somewhere in the 700-hour range, but I didn't find the actual log files. It wasn't just a difference in cpu speed but also an inferior method. Incidentally, out of the 954 extremal graphs for $R(5)$, 24 are self-complementary.

here are some amusing weaker problems where I still do not a know how to get a $\omega(\log(n))$ lower bound. 1) let $\alpha(n)$ be the largest $k$ so that every $n$-vertex graph $G$ contains an induced subgraph $G'$ on at least $k$ vertices, so that all the degrees of $G'$ are equal mod 3 (the analogous problem mod 2 has a linear lower bound via a very slick old argument of Gallai, see also the semi-recent work of Ferber and Krivelevich (link) which shows that if $G$ has minimum degree then you can in fact find an induced subgraph of linear size with all degrees odd); 2) let $\beta(n)$ be the largest $k$ so that every $n$-vertex graph $G$ contains an induced subgraph $G'$, and a set $S\subset V(G')$ of size $k$, all having the same degree in $G'$.
Interesting, thanks Zach. Do you know any non-trivial upper bounds for these?

$\alpha(n)$ (and the higher order modular analogues) should all be linear I believe. meanwhile, $\beta(n)$ has a $O(n/\log(n))$ bound. the construction is take one clique of size $n/\log(n)$, two cliques of size $n/2\log(n)$, 4 cliques of size $n/4\log(n)$, and so on. this was observed in this paper by Caro, Shapira, and Yuster; the same construction also appears in this paper of Noga, Michael, and Benny (where they study $F(n)$ and related problems). I'm inclined to believe this upper bound for $\beta$ is also tight (though I am a little less confident, compared to $\alpha$).

$F(n)$ and $t(n)$ have now been computed up to $n \leq 10$, which seems long enough to merit submission to the OEIS. If there are any Ramsey theory or SAT solver experts who might be able to extend the sequence a little further (or at least confirm the existing data), feel free to help out!
$t(n)$ is in fact known for $n\leq 42$, since $R(4)=18$ and $R(5)\geq 43$ (so that $t(n)=3$ for $6\leq n<18$ and $t(n)=4$ for $18\leq n\leq 42$).
Also this paper by Fajtlowicz, McColgan, Reid, and Staton computes $F(n)$ for $n\leq 11$ (so $F(n)=3$ for $5\leq n\leq 6$ and $F(n)=4$ for $7\leq n\leq 11$).
